title: Learn Trading
subtitle: Everything you need to know to start trading Gold, from zero to confident. Built from expert guides, community wisdom, and professional best practices.

remember.title: REMEMBER
remember.rule1: 1. Never invest more than you can afford to lose
remember.rule2: 2. Always use a stop-loss on every trade
remember.rule3: 3. Start small — learn with amounts that don't cause stress
remember.rule4: 4. Paper trade first — practice without real money until consistent
remember.rule5: 5. The market will always be there tomorrow — no trade is worth your mental health

whatIsBitcoin.title: What is Gold?
whatIsBitcoin.p1: Gold is a precious metal that has served as a store of value and medium of exchange for thousands of years. It is traded globally under the symbol XAU/USD.
whatIsBitcoin.p2_fixedSupplyDesc: (ការផ្គត់ផ្គង់សកលមានកំណត់ ~212,582 តោន),

howTrading.title: How Does Trading Work?
howTrading.p1: Trading means buying and selling Gold to profit from price changes. You buy when you think the price will go up, and sell when you think it will go down.
howTrading.spot_desc: Buy/sell actual Gold. You own the asset.
howTrading.futures_desc: Trade contracts that track Gold's price. Can use leverage (borrowed money) to amplify gains — and losses.

whatMoves.title: What Moves Gold's Price?
whatMoves.supply_desc: More buyers than sellers = price up. More sellers = price down.
whatMoves.halvings_desc: Every ~4 years, the Fed sets interest rates affecting dollar strength and Gold demand.
whatMoves.macro_desc: Interest rates, inflation, dollar strength (DXY), geopolitical events.
whatMoves.sentiment_desc: News, social media, fear & greed. Markets are driven by human emotion.
whatMoves.whales_desc: Large holders (large reserves) moving coins to/from exchanges can signal buying or selling.

marketCap.title: What is Market Cap?
marketCap.p1_desc: = Price x Circulating Supply. It measures the total value of all coins in circulation.
marketCap.p2: Gold has a total market cap of approximately $13 trillion.
marketCap.p3: Higher market cap = more established, lower volatility. Lower market cap = more speculative, higher risk/reward.

wallet.title: What is a Broker Account?
wallet.p1: A broker account is your gateway to trading Gold in the global markets.
wallet.hot_desc: Web-based trading platforms (MT4/MT5, TradingView). Convenient with instant access.
wallet.cold_desc: Physical bars and coins stored in secure vaults. Most secure for large holdings.
wallet.warning: Choose a regulated broker with proper licensing. For large holdings, consider allocated physical gold in secure vaults.

market.title: Market Order
market.cons: Cons: You may get a slightly different price than expected (slippage), especially in volatile markets.
market.useWhen: Use when: You need to enter/exit NOW and price precision doesn't matter much.

limit.title: Limit Order
limit.p1_suffix: at which you want to buy or sell. Only executes if the market reaches that price.
limit.pros: Pros: You control the exact price. No slippage.
limit.cons: Cons: May never fill if price doesn't reach your level.
limit.useWhen: Use when: You have a specific target price and can wait for it.

stopLoss.title: Stop-Loss Order
stopLoss.p1_prefix: Automatically sells when price drops to a set level,
stopLoss.example: Example: Buy at $2,000/oz, set stop-loss at $1,900/oz. If price drops to $95K, it auto-sells. Max loss = $2,000 per oz.
stopLoss.useWhen: Use when: ALWAYS. Every trade should have a stop-loss. No exceptions.

takeProfit.title: Take-Profit Order
takeProfit.example: Example: Buy at $2,000/oz, set take-profit at $2,060/oz. Locks in $6,000 profit per oz.
takeProfit.useWhen: Use when: Always set alongside stop-loss. Have BOTH exit points before entering.

stopLimit.title: Stop-Limit Order
stopLimit.p1_prefix: Combines stop and limit: when price hits the stop price, a
stopLimit.pros: Pros: More price control than a regular stop-loss.
stopLimit.cons: Cons: In fast crashes, the limit order may not fill, leaving you exposed.

trailingStop.title: Trailing Stop
trailingStop.p1_suffix: . Locks in profit as price rises, triggers if price drops by a set amount.
trailingStop.example: Example: Set 3% trailing stop. Price rises from $2,000 to $2,060, stop follows to $1,998. If price drops 3%, it sells at ~$99,910.
trailingStop.useWhen: Use when: You're in a winning trade and want to ride the trend while protecting gains.

rsi.name: RSI (Relative Strength Index)
rsi.desc: Measures momentum on a 0-100 scale. Above 70 = overbought (potential drop). Below 30 = oversold (potential bounce). Most reliable on higher timeframes (4h, 1D).
rsi.tip: Don't buy just because RSI is low. Wait for RSI to turn UP from oversold.

macd.name: MACD
macd.desc: Shows trend direction and momentum. The MACD line crossing above the signal line = bullish. Crossing below = bearish. Histogram shows the gap between the two lines.
macd.tip: MACD + RSI together is the most popular combo. MACD for trend, RSI for timing.

movingAverages.name: Moving Averages (EMA/SMA)
movingAverages.desc: Smooth out price data to show trends. EMA 9 & 21 for short-term, EMA 50 & 200 for long-term. Price above the average = bullish trend.
movingAverages.tip: Golden Cross (50 crosses above 200) = very bullish. Death Cross (50 below 200) = bearish.

bollinger.name: Bollinger Bands
bollinger.desc: Shows volatility range. Price near upper band = potentially overbought. Near lower band = oversold. Bands narrowing = big move coming (breakout).
bollinger.tip: Best used WITH volume. Breakout + high volume = real move. Low volume = likely fakeout.

volume.name: Volume
volume.desc: Number of coins traded. High volume = strong conviction. Low volume = weak move. Volume precedes price — watch for volume spikes before big moves.
volume.tip: Price up + volume up = strong trend. Price up + volume down = weakening, potential reversal.

supportResistance.name: Support & Resistance
supportResistance.desc: Price levels where buying (support) or selling (resistance) pressure is strong. Price bounces off these levels repeatedly.
supportResistance.tip: Once support breaks, it becomes resistance (and vice versa). These are the most important levels to watch.

fibonacci.name: Fibonacci Retracement
fibonacci.desc: Key levels (23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%) where price often bounces during pullbacks. Based on the golden ratio.
fibonacci.tip: The 0.618 level is the most important. If price holds there during a pullback, the trend is likely intact.

ichimoku.name: Ichimoku Cloud
ichimoku.desc: All-in-one indicator showing support/resistance, trend direction, and momentum. Price above cloud = bullish. Below = bearish.
ichimoku.tip: The cloud itself acts as support/resistance. Thick cloud = strong. Thin cloud = weak, breakout likely.

fearGreed.name: Fear & Greed Index
fearGreed.desc: Market sentiment score 0-100. Extreme Fear (0-25) = potential buy. Extreme Greed (75-100) = potential sell. Uses volatility, volume, social media, and dominance.
fearGreed.tip: "Be fearful when others are greedy, greedy when others are fearful" — Warren Buffett.

fundingRate.name: Funding Rate
fundingRate.desc: Shows if the derivatives market is over-leveraged long or short. High positive = too many longs (bearish signal). Negative = too many shorts (bullish signal).
fundingRate.tip: Extreme funding rates often precede liquidation cascades and sharp reversals.

hodl.title: HODLing (Buy & Hold)
hodl.p1: The simplest strategy. Buy Gold and hold for years regardless of short-term price swings.
hodl.bestFor_desc: Beginners. Historically, anyone who held Gold for 4+ years has been profitable.
hodl.risk_desc: Low (if you can handle seeing -50% drops without panic selling).

dca.title: Dollar Cost Averaging (DCA)
dca.p1: Invest a fixed amount at regular intervals (weekly/monthly) regardless of price. Removes the stress of timing the market.
dca.bestFor_desc: Beginners and long-term investors. Proven to outperform most timing strategies.
dca.example_desc: $100/week for 2 years. You buy more Gold when cheap, less when expensive. Average cost smooths out.

swing.title: Swing Trading
swing.p1: Hold positions for days to weeks, capturing medium-term price swings using technical analysis.
swing.bestFor_desc: Intermediate traders. Requires understanding of charts, indicators, and risk management.
swing.tools_desc: Support/resistance, RSI, MACD, volume. Enter at support, exit at resistance.

day.title: Day Trading
day.p1: Open and close positions within the same day. Requires constant monitoring and fast execution.
day.bestFor_desc: Advanced traders only. 95% of day traders lose money — this is a statistical fact.
day.warning_desc: High stress, high fees, requires discipline. Start with paper trading (practice with fake money).

onePercent.title: The 1% Risk Rule
onePercent.p1: Never risk more than 1% of your total portfolio on a single trade. This is THE most important rule in trading.
onePercent.example_desc: $10,000 account = max $100 risk per trade. If your stop-loss is 2% below entry, your position size = $5,000.
onePercent.p2: With the 1% rule, you can lose 10 trades in a row and only be down 10%. Without it, one bad trade can destroy your account.

srTrading.title: Support/Resistance Trading
srTrading.p1: Identify key price levels where Gold repeatedly bounces (support) or gets rejected (resistance).
srTrading.strategy_desc: Buy near support with stop-loss just below. Sell near resistance or set take-profit there.
srTrading.confirmation_desc: Wait for a bounce (don't buy into a falling knife). Volume + RSI turning up = stronger signal.

onePercent.title: The 1% Rule (Expanded)
onePercent.p1_suffix: on any single trade. This is the foundation of survival in trading.
onePercent.example_account: Account: $10,000. Max risk per trade: $10,000 x 0.01 =
onePercent.tip: Even 10 consecutive losses = only -10%. You survive. Without the 1% rule, one bad trade can end your career.

riskReward.title: Risk-to-Reward Ratio
riskReward.p1: The ratio between how much you risk (stop-loss distance) and how much you stand to gain (take-profit distance).
riskReward.minimum_desc: — risk $1 to make $2. This means you only need to win 34% of trades to be profitable.
riskReward.tip: Never take a trade with R:R below 1:1.5. If the setup doesn't offer good R:R, skip it — there will always be another trade.

stopLoss.title: Stop-Loss Placement
stopLoss.p1: Where you place your stop-loss matters more than whether you use one. A badly placed stop gets hit by normal market noise.
stopLoss.belowSupport_desc: Place stop just below a key support level, not at an arbitrary %. If support is at $1,900/oz, set stop at $1,895/oz.
stopLoss.atr_desc: Use the Average True Range indicator. Stop = Entry - (1.5 x ATR). This adapts to current volatility.
stopLoss.neverDo_desc: "I'll set my stop at -5% because that feels right." Random percentages have no relation to market structure.
stopLoss.tip: A good stop-loss is placed where your trade thesis is invalidated — if price reaches that level, your reason for entering no longer holds.

positionSizing.title: Position Sizing
positionSizing.p1: The exact formula to calculate how big your position should be:
positionSizing.formula: Position Size = Risk Amount / (Entry Price - Stop-Loss Price)
positionSizing.example_note: This means you're using ~65% of your account but only risking 1%. The stop-loss controls the risk, not the position size alone.

portfolioHeat.title: Portfolio Heat
portfolioHeat.p1_suffix: = the total risk across ALL your open positions combined.
portfolioHeat.max_desc: 6% total portfolio risk at any time.
portfolioHeat.p3: If you risk 1% per trade, that means maximum 6 open trades. But if your trades are correlated (e.g., all precious metals), the real risk is higher.
portfolioHeat.correlated_desc: If you're long Gold and long Silver, those aren't truly separate bets — they tend to move together. Count them as 1.5-2x risk.
portfolioHeat.tip: Most blown accounts come from having too many positions open at once during a market crash. Portfolio heat is your defense.

leverage.title: Leverage Dangers
leverage.p1: Leverage amplifies both gains AND losses. Here's the math most people ignore:
leverage.2x: 2x leverage: 50% drop = liquidation. Gold drops 50% roughly once per cycle.
leverage.5x: 5x leverage: 20% drop = liquidation. Gold drops 20% several times per year.
leverage.10x: 10x leverage: 10% drop = liquidation. Gold moves 10% in a single week regularly.
leverage.25x: 25x leverage: 4% drop = liquidation. This can happen in minutes.
leverage.100x: 100x leverage: 1% drop = liquidation. You are gambling, not trading.
leverage.safe: Safe leverage: 2-5x maximum, with tight stop-losses. Most professional traders use 1-3x. If you need high leverage to make money, your position sizing is wrong.

drawdown.title: Drawdown Recovery
drawdown.p1_prefix: Losses are not symmetrical. Recovering from a loss requires a
drawdown.tip: This is why protecting capital is more important than making gains. A 50% loss requires a 100% gain just to break even. Prevention > cure.

fomo.title: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out)
fomo.p1: That painful feeling when price is pumping and you're not in the trade. Your brain screams "BUY NOW before it goes higher!"
fomo.whyDangerous_desc: FOMO makes you buy at the top. The best entries happen when the market is quiet and boring — not when everyone is excited.
fomo.howToFight_1: 1. Remind yourself: if you missed the move, you missed the move. There will be another one.
fomo.howToFight_2: 2. Never chase a green candle. Wait for a pullback to support before entering.
fomo.howToFight_3: 3. If you feel intense urgency to buy, that's usually the worst time to buy.
fomo.tip: The market is open 24/7, 365 days a year. There is ALWAYS another opportunity. Missing one trade is nothing — buying the top is everything.

revenge.title: Revenge Trading
revenge.p1: You just lost money. You feel angry, frustrated, humiliated. Your instinct is to immediately open a bigger trade to "win it back."
revenge.whyKiller_desc: Revenge trades are emotional, not strategic. You increase size, ignore your plan, and usually lose again — now even more. This spiral has destroyed more accounts than any market crash.
revenge.howToFight_1_suffix: (e.g., max 3% per day). Hit it = done for the day. No exceptions.
revenge.howToFight_2: 2. After a loss, take at least 30 minutes away from the screen.
revenge.howToFight_3: 3. Never increase position size after a loss. If anything, reduce it.
revenge.tip: Professional traders have a rule: after 2-3 consecutive losses, stop trading for the day. The market will be there tomorrow. Your capital might not be.

confirmation.title: Confirmation Bias
confirmation.p1: When you have a position, your brain automatically filters information to support your view and ignores evidence against it.
confirmation.example_desc: You're long Gold. You read 10 bullish tweets and feel great. You see a bearish analysis and dismiss it as "FUD." Meanwhile, the bearish analysis was right.
confirmation.howToFight_1: 1. Actively seek out the opposite view before entering any trade.
confirmation.howToFight_2: 2. Ask yourself: "What would make this trade wrong?" Write that down.
confirmation.howToFight_3: 3. Follow analysts who disagree with you — they'll catch what you miss.
confirmation.tip: The best traders are always looking for reasons they might be wrong. The worst traders only look for reasons they're right.

overtrading.title: Overtrading
overtrading.p1: More trades does NOT mean more profit. Each trade has fees, slippage, and emotional cost. Most traders would be more profitable making fewer, higher-quality trades.
overtrading.signs_1: - Trading out of boredom when there's no clear setup
overtrading.signs_2: - Opening positions just to "be in the market"
overtrading.signs_3: - Feeling anxious when you don't have an open trade
overtrading.signs_4: - Taking setups that don't meet all your criteria
overtrading.fix_desc: Quality over quantity. 2-3 high-conviction trades per week beat 20 random trades. If there's no clear setup, doing nothing IS a valid position.

tradingPlan.title: The Trading Plan
tradingPlan.p1_suffix: you enter the trade, while you're thinking clearly. Once money is on the line, emotions take over.
tradingPlan.must_1_desc: Why are you entering? What's the setup?
tradingPlan.must_5_desc: Based on 1% rule and stop distance.
tradingPlan.must_6_desc: When do you stop for the day?
tradingPlan.tip: If you can't write a plan for a trade, don't take the trade. "I just feel like it'll go up" is not a plan.

detachment.title: Emotional Detachment
detachment.p1: Every trade is a probability, not a certainty. Even the best setups fail 30-40% of the time. Treating trades as statistics instead of personal wins/losses is the key to longevity.
detachment.mindset_1: - Don't say "I lost" — say "The trade was stopped out." It's not personal.
detachment.mindset_2: - Don't celebrate winning trades — you might have won for the wrong reason.
detachment.mindset_3: - Focus on whether you followed your plan, not the outcome. A losing trade executed well is better than a winning trade based on luck.
detachment.tip: Think of yourself as a casino, not a gambler. The casino doesn't celebrate every hand — it trusts the math over thousands of hands.

whenNot.title: When NOT to Trade
whenNot.p1: Some of the best trades are the ones you don't take. Knowing when to stay out is a skill:
whenNot.dontTrade_2: - You're emotional (angry, euphoric, stressed, or sad)
whenNot.dontTrade_4: - Right after a big loss (revenge trading urge)
whenNot.dontTrade_5: - There's no clear setup in the market
whenNot.dontTrade_6: - Major uncertain news events are about to drop (FOMC, CPI)
whenNot.dontTrade_7: - You're checking your P&L every 30 seconds
whenNot.tip: The market rewards patience. Sitting in cash waiting for a perfect setup IS trading. The best traders spend most of their time doing nothing.

sr.title: Support & Resistance
sr.support_desc: = a price level where buying pressure is strong enough to prevent the price from falling further. The price "bounces" off this level.
sr.resistance_desc: = a price level where selling pressure prevents the price from rising further. The price gets "rejected" here.
sr.rules_1: 1. The more times a level is tested, the stronger it is.
sr.rules_2: 2. When support breaks, it becomes resistance (and vice versa) — this is called a "flip."
sr.rules_3: 3. Round numbers ($1,500, $2,000) act as psychological S/R levels.
sr.rules_4: 4. S/R levels are zones, not exact prices. Think $1,895/oz-$1,905/oz, not $1,900/oz exactly.
sr.tip: S/R is the single most important concept in trading. Master this before anything else.

doubleTopBottom.title: Double Top / Double Bottom
doubleTopBottom.p1_suffix: where price tests the same level twice and fails to break through.
doubleTopBottom.doubleTop_desc: Price hits resistance twice, forming an "M" shape. Signals a potential drop. Sell/short when price breaks below the neckline (the low between the two peaks).
doubleTopBottom.doubleBottom_desc: Price hits support twice, forming a "W" shape. Signals a potential rise. Buy when price breaks above the neckline (the high between the two dips).
doubleTopBottom.target_desc: Measure the distance from peak to neckline — that's your expected move from the breakout point.
doubleTopBottom.tip: Wait for the neckline break with volume confirmation. Many double tops/bottoms fail — the break is the signal, not the second touch.

headShoulders.title: Head & Shoulders
headShoulders.p1_suffix: . Three peaks: left shoulder, head (highest), right shoulder.
headShoulders.regular_desc: After an uptrend. The neckline connects the lows between the three peaks. Break below the neckline = sell signal.
headShoulders.inverse_desc: After a downtrend. Three troughs with the middle being the lowest. Break above the neckline = buy signal.
headShoulders.target_desc: Distance from head to neckline, projected from the breakout point.
headShoulders.tip: The neckline doesn't have to be perfectly horizontal — it can be slightly angled. Volume should decrease on the right shoulder and spike on the neckline break.

triangles.title: Triangles (Ascending, Descending, Symmetrical)
triangles.p1: Price consolidates between converging trendlines, building pressure before a breakout.
triangles.ascending_desc: Flat top resistance + rising lower trendline. Buyers are getting more aggressive. Usually breaks upward.
triangles.descending_desc: Flat bottom support + falling upper trendline. Sellers are getting more aggressive. Usually breaks downward.
triangles.symmetrical_desc: Both trendlines converging equally. Can break either way — wait for direction confirmation.
triangles.entry_desc: Trade the breakout direction with a stop-loss on the other side of the triangle.
triangles.tip: Breakouts in the last 25% of the triangle (near the apex) are weaker. The best breakouts happen in the first 50-75% of the pattern.

flags.title: Flags & Pennants
flags.p1_prefix: Short consolidation patterns after a strong, sharp move (the "flagpole"). They signal
flags.bullFlag_desc: Strong move up (flagpole), then price drifts down in a small parallel channel (flag). Breakout upward continues the move.
flags.bearFlag_desc: Strong move down, then price drifts up in a small channel. Breakout downward continues the move.
flags.pennant_desc: Same as a flag but the consolidation forms a small symmetrical triangle instead of a channel.
flags.target_desc: Measure the flagpole length and project it from the breakout point.
flags.tip: Flags should form quickly (5-15 candles). If consolidation takes too long, the pattern weakens and may not work.

cup.title: Cup & Handle
cup.p1_suffix: pattern that looks like a tea cup from the side. Usually forms over weeks to months.
cup.cup_desc: A U-shaped (not V-shaped) decline and recovery. Price drops, rounds out at a bottom, and recovers back to the starting level.
cup.handle_desc: A small pullback/consolidation near the right side of the cup. This is the final shakeout before breakout.
cup.entry_desc: Buy when price breaks above the rim of the cup (the resistance level) with volume.
cup.target_desc: Depth of the cup projected upward from the breakout.
cup.tip: The handle should NOT drop more than 50% of the cup's depth. If it does, the pattern is weakening.

candlestick.title: Candlestick Basics
candlestick.p1: Individual candles can signal reversals or continuation. Here are the most important ones:
candlestick.doji_desc: Open and close at nearly the same price. Body is tiny or nonexistent. Shows indecision — neither buyers nor sellers won. At the top of a trend = potential reversal.
candlestick.hammer_desc: Small body at the top, long lower wick (2x+ body length). Appears at bottoms — buyers rejected the low. Bullish reversal signal.
candlestick.shootingStar_desc: Small body at the bottom, long upper wick. Appears at tops — sellers rejected the high. Bearish reversal signal.
candlestick.engulfing_desc: A candle that completely covers the previous candle's body. Bullish engulfing = green candle swallows red. Bearish engulfing = red swallows green. Strong reversal signal.
candlestick.tip: Single candles are weak signals alone. Always combine with S/R levels and volume. A hammer AT support with high volume = strong. A hammer in the middle of nowhere = meaningless.

glossary.ATH: All-Time High — the highest price ever reached
glossary.ATL: All-Time Low — the lowest price ever recorded
glossary.Candle: A chart bar showing open, high, low, close price for a time period
glossary.DCA: Dollar Cost Averaging — buying fixed amounts at regular intervals
glossary.DeFi: Derivatives Finance — សេវាហិរញ្ញវត្ថុ derivatives នៅលើទីផ្សារមាស
glossary.DYOR: Do Your Own Research — never blindly follow others' advice
glossary.FOMO: Fear Of Missing Out — emotional buying during rallies
glossary.FUD: Fear, Uncertainty, Doubt — negative news/sentiment (sometimes manufactured)
glossary.Gas: Transaction fees paid to market participants
glossary.Halving: Federal Reserve meeting every 6 weeks that sets interest rates
glossary.HODL: Hold On for Dear Life — long-term holding strategy
glossary.Leverage: Borrowing money to trade larger positions (amplifies gains AND losses)
glossary.Liquidation: Forced closing of a leveraged position when losses exceed margin
glossary.Long: A trade betting the price will go UP
glossary.MACD: Moving Average Convergence Divergence — trend/momentum indicator
glossary.MarketCap: Total value = Price x Circulating Supply
glossary.MVRV: Market Value to Realized Value — fundamental valuation metric
glossary.OI: Open Interest — total value of open futures/derivatives contracts
glossary.Satoshi: One troy ounce — standard unit for measuring gold weight (31.1 grams).
glossary.Short: A trade betting the price will go DOWN
glossary.Slippage: Difference between expected and actual execution price
glossary.StopLoss: Order that auto-sells if price drops to a set level
glossary.Support: Price level where buying pressure prevents further drops
glossary.Resistance: Price level where selling pressure prevents further rises
glossary.TakeProfit: Order that auto-sells when price reaches your profit target
glossary.TVL: Total assets under management in Gold ETFs and funds
glossary.Whale: អង្គភាពដែលកាន់កាប់មាសចំនួនច្រើន (សេរ៉ូធំ)
